splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA's statutory measurement date requires all actuarial assumptions to be locked in by year-end or whether actuaries retain discretion to select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential withdrawal liability obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions used to calculate withdrawal liability must be determined as of the statutory measurement date (end of prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects revocation proceedings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the supervised release term is tolled while a supervisee is a fugitive, so the clock stops running during the period of absconding.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to create such a common-law doctrine that extends punishment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion, in addition to § 2255(h)'s separate requirements. The answer determines how strictly federal prisoners are gatekept when seeking successive collateral attacks on their convictions.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, categorically barring any claim previously presented.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the standard for when candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The resolution affects the ability to challenge mail-in voting rules, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices nationwide.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have a cognizable competitive injury sufficient for Article III standing when a state law allegedly dilutes or affects the counting of votes in their race.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized interest in election rules does not constitute a concrete, particularized injury sufficient for Article III standing to challenge ballot-counting procedures pre-election.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous provisions in a criminal or quasi-criminal context, such as Sentencing Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps agency deference when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines enhancements with criminal consequences. At least three circuits hold lenity prevails, while others (including the Third Circuit panel in Chandler) have held that deference trumps lenity. Post-Loper Bright, this tension has intensified.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal or sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes precedence over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions, deferring to the agency's anti-defendant reading.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means all three criminal-history criteria must be met to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any single criterion is disqualifying. This affects whether defendants can be sentenced below statutory minimums and implicates liberty interests across different federal districts.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive (all three required to disqualify)
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies)
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) encompasses inchoate offenses like conspiracy, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation of the guideline or impermissibly expands its text. This directly affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) under Stinson, and conspiracy convictions qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the plain text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly adds to rather than interprets the guideline, especially under Kisor's narrowed deference framework.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for a sentence reduction under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree sharply on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive statutory changes as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others permit courts to consider them. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) further deepened the conflict by authorizing such consideration, which some circuits have rejected as exceeding the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons under § 3582(c)(1)(A), and the Sentencing Commission lacks authority to override this through policy statements.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule includes substances not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled, or whether any state-law drug conviction qualifies regardless of federal scheduling. This affects the career-offender enhancement and base offense level calculations for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: No federal-schedule limitation
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b)'s reference to 'an offense under ... state law' does not require the controlled substance to also be federally scheduled; any state drug offense qualifies.

positions.label: Federal-schedule limitation applies
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a controlled substance offense only if the substance is also controlled under federal law, applying a categorical approach that compares state and federal schedules.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. De La Rosa

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to the conditions or execution of their confinement through habeas corpus, or whether they may also pursue such claims through civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens or the APA).
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists over whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy when prisoners challenge the execution of their sentences — such as BOP decisions on prerelease custody — or whether civil rights and statutory claims remain available. This affects the procedural vehicle federal prisoners must use and the scope of judicial review available.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for execution-of-sentence claims
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting the fact or duration of confinement, including prerelease custody determinations, must be brought exclusively through habeas corpus.

positions.label: Civil rights actions are also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners challenging conditions or execution of their confinement may proceed via civil rights actions or APA claims, and habeas is not the exclusive remedy when the relief sought goes beyond release.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Carmona v. U.S. Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the 30-day civil deadline) or a criminal matter (triggering the shorter 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case and the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the Chapter 13 filing date and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. This has significant consequences for debtors who convert in good faith but whose assets have appreciated during the pendency of the Chapter 13 case.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A) and § 541(a), post-petition appreciation is 'property of the estate' and belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects good-faith converting debtors by limiting the estate to the value of property as of the original petition date, so post-petition appreciation inures to the debtor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose sentences upon revocation of supervised release that are based on punishing the defendant for the violation of supervised release conditions, or only on the factors enumerated in 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e).
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit's precedent in United States v. Lewis allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split. Dissenting judges have flagged that this approach contravenes the statutory text and Supreme Court precedent, and that other circuits limit revocation sentences to the factors in § 3583(e).

positions.label: Punishment for violation is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly impose revocation sentences to punish defendants for violating conditions of supervised release.

positions.label: Revocation sentence should be based on breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of revocation sentencing is to sanction the breach of trust inherent in a supervised release violation, not to punish the new criminal conduct, and the sentence must be based on the § 3583(e) factors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that denials of motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that is effectively unreviewable after final judgment. Other circuits have taken a narrower approach to the collateral order doctrine in the sealing context, creating a potential split on when privacy-based sealing denials qualify.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because the privacy interest in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of high order effectively unreviewable after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that sealing orders generally are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine, as other remedies may adequately protect privacy interests and piecemeal appeals should be disfavored.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or related civil rights statutes?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in hospitality settings, indicating a circuit split on the applicable legal standards for such claims. The case will clarify standards for fair treatment, discrimination liability, and damages in service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied a particular legal framework to discrimination claims arising from hospitality establishments that the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Differing approach by other circuits
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards to discrimination claims in commercial or hospitality contexts, creating the split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any... broker,' preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers arising from accidents involving the motor carriers they selected. This affects liability standards across the trucking and logistics industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because broker selection of carriers is a 'service' related to the arrangement of transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent selection of carriers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they do not relate to brokers' prices, routes, or services as those terms are properly construed.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances, particularly marijuana, is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and the nation's historical tradition of firearms regulation.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that the historical tradition of disarming those who pose a danger to themselves or others, including habitual substance users, supports the constitutionality of § 922(g)(3) even after Bruen.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government cannot demonstrate a sufficient historical analogue for disarming non-violent drug users.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may serve as a vehicle for raising claims that could also be brought under § 2255 habeas petitions, such as claims of legal error at sentencing. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used to circumvent § 2255's procedural requirements.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claims related to errors in conviction or sentencing as one factor in the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used to raise claims of legal error that are properly the subject of § 2255 motions, as this would circumvent habeas procedural requirements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'engaged in . . . interstate commerce.' This affects the enforceability of arbitration agreements for a wide range of distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are part of a continuous interstate transportation chain, including those making last-mile deliveries of goods that have moved in interstate commerce, qualify as transportation workers exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that distribution workers delivering goods locally to retail stores are not 'transportation workers engaged in . . . interstate commerce' under Section 1 because they are not themselves moving goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
